evals.prompt: Our Shopify CVR was ~2.8% last month and this week it's ~1.9% with similar traffic. Nothing obvious broke on our site. I noticed Competitor X emailed a 30% off sitewide on Monday. How do I know if that's the cause and what should we do about pricing?
evals.expected_output: Timeline/correlation guidance; hypothesis stack (competitor vs other causes); required 4+ row matrix (move, metric, confidence, lever, guardrail); price-match or value-defense options with margin/kill-switch thinking; not only 'match 30%.'
expectations: Includes a Markdown table with columns for competitor move, your metric shift (hypothesis), confidence, response lever, and guardrail or kill switch
Table has at least four rows
Discusses aligning competitor promo timing with conversion or funnel metrics and notes attribution or mix caveats
Lists non-competitor explanations for CVR drop (e.g. creative, stock, seasonality) alongside competitor hypothesis
Proposes concrete response levers beyond a blind sitewide price cut (e.g. targeted match, bundle, loyalty, service)
Mentions margin floors, duration limits, or exit criteria for any promotional response

evals.prompt: We're drafting a price match guarantee for our DTC store. MAP from the brand says we can't advertise below $89 but competitors sometimes show $79 on Google Shopping. Legal said keep it high level. Give me operational rules and customer-facing principles.
evals.expected_output: Eligibility, proof, channel/MAP tension called out (without legal advice), stacking, caps, logging; matrix still useful for mapping scenarios vs responses; clear that MAP needs brand/legal alignment.
expectations: Addresses price match policy elements such as eligibility, proof of competitor price, and channel considerations
Acknowledges MAP or minimum advertised price tension and recommends coordinating with brand policy or counsel without claiming to be legal advice
Includes operational guardrails (caps, stacking, who approves exceptions)
Includes a structured matrix or equivalent scenario rows mapping competitor listing types to allowed responses
Customer-facing principles emphasize trust and fairness rather than aggressive discounting tone

evals.prompt: Write 10 blog post titles for our skincare brand spring campaign. No pricing or competitors mentioned.
evals.expected_output: Brief creative SEO/content help; does NOT dump the full competitor-offer monitoring template or mandatory CVR matrix.
expectations: Does not use the full competitor-offer response matrix as the primary deliverable
Does not force price match, CVR diagnostics, or competitor monitoring workflow for a pure content request
May give blog titles or ask clarifying questions appropriate to content marketing
